text: Last class you condensed the report into a clear executive summary.
Today you turn that summary into a proposal that earns a yes.
Rationale
Compelling
Persuade
Incentive
Endorse
Justify
Upside
Viable
If you approve the pilot, we will deliver results in thirty days.
Should you decide to proceed, we will assign a dedicated team.
Were we to partner, your onboarding time would fall by half.
If you sign this quarter, we will waive the setup fee.
Here is my draft. It opens with: We offer a B2B platform with many features at a competitive price.
That describes us, but it does not persuade. Where is the rationale? Start with why this matters to the partner.
Right. The rationale is that they lose two weeks per onboarding. So the upside is: if you approve the pilot, we will cut that time in half.
Much more compelling. Now lower the barrier with an incentive, and make sure the promise is viable.
The incentive: if you sign this quarter, we will waive the setup fee. And I close by asking him to endorse the pilot.
Now that is a proposal. Same facts as the summary, but it gives the partner a reason to say yes. Send it.
The rationale is simple: this matters to your bottom line.
I would be glad to have you endorse the next step.

Hi Eduardo, it is Sarah. Last week you wrote the executive summary, and it was clear. Now I need the next step: turn that summary into a proposal that actually wins the partner over. A summary just informs; a proposal has to persuade. So lead with the rationale, the reason this matters to them, not to us. Make the benefit compelling, and make it about their upside. Then use a persuasive if: if they approve the pilot, we will onboard their team within thirty days. Add an incentive, something that makes saying yes easy, like a reduced fee for the first quarter. Keep it viable, nothing we cannot deliver. Close by asking them to endorse the next step. Remember, the partner does not want a report. He wants a reason to say yes.

Let me think about how to persuade before I write a single line. A weak proposal lists what we do. A strong one gives the reader a reason to act. So I start with the rationale: why this matters to them. Then I make the benefit compelling and concrete, framed as their upside, not our revenue. The persuasive part is the conditional. If you approve this pilot, we will deliver the first results in thirty days. Should you decide to proceed, we will assign a dedicated team. Each if opens a door and shows what is on the other side. I add one incentive to lower the barrier, I keep every promise viable, and I ask the partner to endorse the plan. A proposal is not a description. It is an argument designed to earn a yes.

First, you open with the rationale, the reason the proposal matters to the reader. Then you make the benefit compelling and frame it as their upside. After that, you use a persuasive if: if you approve this, we will deliver. Next, you add one incentive that makes saying yes easy. Finally, you ask the reader to endorse the next step.
